title: Designing a Supplier Audit Programme
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How to design a risk-based supplier audit programme that satisfies the MHRA: scope, ICH Q9 risk tiering, scheduling, CAPA closure and meaningful metrics.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a subtle green accent. A quality professional at a clean, polished workstation reviews a multi-year supplier audit schedule and a risk-tiering matrix on a tablet, with softly blurred glass vials, a stainless-steel surface and a calendar wall display in the background. Soft natural light from a side window, shallow depth of field with crisp focus on the tablet and hands, calm and methodical mood, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a supplier audit and a supplier audit programme?
faqs.a: A supplier audit is a single, point-in-time evaluation of one supplier against your quality requirements. A supplier audit programme is the overarching system that governs all such audits: the policy, risk criteria, schedule, responsibilities and lifecycle that decide who is audited, how deeply, how often and what happens to the findings. Inspectors assess the programme, because it is the evidence of continuous, systematic oversight rather than ad hoc activity.

faqs.q: How do I decide how often to audit each supplier?
faqs.a: Frequency should be driven by the supplier's risk tier rather than a single fixed interval, in line with ICH Q9. Critical suppliers such as sterile manufacturers, active substance makers and contract laboratories typically warrant periodic on-site audits, while lower-risk suppliers may be covered by extended intervals, remote assessment or questionnaires. In addition, defined triggers such as serious deviations, major changes or adverse inspection outcomes should prompt a for-cause audit outside the routine cycle.

faqs.q: What records does the MHRA expect to see for a supplier audit programme?
faqs.a: Expect to show a written audit policy, your risk-tiering rationale for each supplier, a current multi-year audit schedule with evidence of adherence, and individual audit reports with classified findings. You should also demonstrate verified CAPA closure, links to quality and technical agreements, and that supplier performance feeds management review under ICH Q10. The aim is a closed loop from risk assessment through audit to CAPA and re-assessment that an inspector can follow end to end.
